params.text:
Breaking tasks down

Large or multi-step tasks can feel overwhelming, particularly where memory and organisation are affected. Breaking a task into smaller, clear steps makes it far more manageable and reduces the chance of something being missed. Giving one instruction at a time, and checking understanding before moving on, helps a person keep track without feeling lost or rushed.

  • Small steps: break tasks into clear, single steps rather than one long list.
  • One at a time: give instructions one by one and allow time to take each in.
  • Check in: gently confirm understanding before moving on, without testing the person.

Avoiding putting people on the spot

Being asked to read aloud, fill in a form in front of others, or answer quickly can be deeply uncomfortable for someone with dyslexia. Putting a person on the spot can trigger anxiety and embarrassment, and may cause them to avoid situations altogether. Thoughtful care means never singling someone out or making them perform under pressure.

  • Never force reading aloud: do not expect anyone to read in front of others unless they choose to.
  • Offer privacy: help with forms or paperwork discreetly, away from an audience.
  • Give warning and time: let people prepare rather than springing tasks on them.

These small courtesies protect a person’s dignity and make it far easier for them to accept the help they need.
